sections.title: Structure and function of the GI tract

cards.front: Which enteric plexus lies in the submucosa, and what two functions does it control?
cards.back: The submucosal (Meissner) plexus sits in the submucosa and controls glandular secretion and local blood flow. It is the plexus that communicates with the glands.

cards.front: Which enteric plexus lies between the circular and longitudinal muscle layers, and what does it govern?
cards.back: The myenteric (Auerbach) plexus lies between the inner circular and outer longitudinal muscle of the muscularis externa and controls motility. It is the plexus that communicates with muscle.

cards.front: Why can a patient with a complete spinal cord transection above the sacral outflow still digest food?
cards.back: The enteric nervous system functions autonomously, so digestion continues even when the CNS connection is lost. The CNS only modulates how vigorously the gut works, it does not initiate digestion.

cards.front: Which extrinsic nerves provide parasympathetic input to the GI tract, and how does their territory divide?
cards.back: The vagus nerve supplies parasympathetic input from the esophagus through the proximal two-thirds of the colon, while the pelvic nerves (S2-S4) cover the distal one-third of the colon, rectum, and internal anal sphincter. Parasympathetic activity increases motility and secretion and relaxes sphincters.

cards.front: How does sympathetic innervation affect the gut, and why does this make GI ischemia a feature of shock?
cards.back: Sympathetic input (T5-L2 via splanchnic nerves) decreases motility and secretion, contracts sphincters, and vasoconstricts splanchnic vessels. That splanchnic vasoconstriction is why GI ischemia occurs in shock states.

cards.front: In the cellular adaptation cascade, what defines metaplasia and is it reversible?
cards.back: Metaplasia is the replacement of one mature cell type by another mature cell type better suited to the stress. It is reversible if the inciting stressor is removed.

cards.front: What distinguishes dysplasia from metaplasia, and how is high-grade dysplasia treated?
cards.back: Dysplasia is replacement tissue that shows disordered growth and nuclear atypia. It may still be reversible early, but high-grade dysplasia is treated as pre-malignant.

cards.front: What histologic event marks the transition from dysplasia to carcinoma, and is it reversible?
cards.back: Carcinoma is defined by frank invasion through the basement membrane. This step is irreversible.

cards.front: What muscle type forms the upper esophageal sphincter, and what dysphagia results from its failure?
cards.back: The upper esophageal sphincter is skeletal muscle responsible for voluntary initiation of the swallow. Failure here causes oropharyngeal dysphagia.

cards.front: What innervation relaxes the lower esophageal sphincter, and what happens when those neurons are lost?
cards.back: The lower esophageal sphincter is smooth muscle relaxed by inhibitory NO/VIP signaling from myenteric (Auerbach) neurons. Loss of these inhibitory neurons prevents LES relaxation, producing achalasia.

cards.front: What pattern of dysphagia points to a motility disorder rather than a mechanical one, and what is the classic example?
cards.back: Motility dysphagia causes trouble with both solids and liquids from the start, due to failure of coordinated peristalsis or sphincter relaxation. The classic example is achalasia.

cards.front: What dysphagia pattern suggests mechanical obstruction, and what conditions classically cause it?
cards.back: Obstructive dysphagia causes trouble with solids first that progresses to liquids as the lumen narrows. Classic causes include esophageal cancer, peptic stricture, Schatzki ring, and esophageal web.

cards.front: What features distinguish B12 deficiency glossitis from other causes, and what separates it from folate deficiency?
cards.back: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes a smooth, beefy-red tongue with megaloblastic anemia and neurologic symptoms such as subacute combined degeneration. The neurologic involvement is what distinguishes B12 from folate deficiency.

cards.front: What oral and nail findings accompany iron deficiency anemia, and what syndrome results when an esophageal web is added?
cards.back: Iron deficiency anemia causes atrophic glossitis, angular cheilitis, and koilonychia. Adding an upper esophageal web produces Plummer-Vinson syndrome.

cards.front: What tongue finding and clinical context suggest scarlet fever, and what organism is responsible?
cards.back: Scarlet fever causes a strawberry tongue in a child with pharyngitis and a sandpaper rash. It is caused by group A streptococcus.

cards.front: Which vitamin deficiency causes pellagra, and what are its classic clinical features?
cards.back: Niacin (B3) deficiency causes pellagra, characterized by the four D's: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, and death, with glossitis as a feature.

cards.front: How does achalasia classically present?
cards.back: Achalasia presents with dysphagia to both solids and liquids from onset, regurgitation of undigested food, and weight loss.

cards.front: What is the classic barium swallow finding in achalasia?
cards.back: Barium swallow shows the classic bird's beak appearance, a dilated esophagus tapering to a narrowed lower esophageal sphincter.

cards.front: What test confirms achalasia, and what findings establish the diagnosis?
cards.back: Esophageal manometry confirms achalasia, showing incomplete LES relaxation and aperistalsis. Manometry is the gold standard.

cards.front: How does GERD typically present, and what is a reasonable initial management step?
cards.back: GERD presents with heartburn and regurgitation that worsen when supine or after meals. An empiric trial of a proton pump inhibitor is reasonable initial management.

cards.front: Which alarm features in a patient with reflux symptoms mandate endoscopy?
cards.back: Dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, anemia, hematemesis, and new symptoms in a patient older than 50 are alarm features. Any of these mandates endoscopy.

cards.front: What defines Barrett's esophagus histologically, and how is the diagnosis confirmed?
cards.back: Barrett's esophagus is intestinal metaplasia of the distal esophagus, confirmed on biopsy by the presence of goblet cells. Patients undergo surveillance endoscopy at defined intervals.

cards.front: What is the feared endpoint of the Barrett's cascade, and what are its risk factors?
cards.back: Esophageal adenocarcinoma of the distal third of the esophagus is the feared endpoint. Risk factors include chronic GERD, obesity, smoking, and male sex.

cards.front: What is a sliding (Type I) hiatal hernia, and how is it managed?
cards.back: In a sliding hiatal hernia, which accounts for about 95% of cases, the GE junction slides up into the chest through the hiatus and presents with reflux symptoms. It is managed medically with PPIs and lifestyle changes.

cards.front: What anatomy defines a paraesophageal hiatal hernia, and why may surgery be indicated even when asymptomatic?
cards.back: In a paraesophageal hernia (Type II-IV, about 5%), the GE junction stays in place while the fundus herniates alongside the esophagus through the hiatus. Surgical repair may be warranted even when asymptomatic in selected cases because of the risk of strangulation, volvulus, and ischemia.

cards.front: How does the relationship of pain to food differ between duodenal and gastric ulcers, and what is the dominant cause of both?
cards.back: Duodenal ulcer pain is relieved by food as acid is neutralized, whereas gastric ulcer pain is worsened by food. H. pylori is the dominant cause of both, with NSAIDs second.

cards.front: What distinguishes Crohn's disease from ulcerative colitis in distribution, depth, and complications?
cards.back: Crohn's disease can affect anywhere from mouth to anus with skip lesions, is transmural, causes fistulas, and classically causes RLQ pain. Ulcerative colitis extends continuously from the rectum upward, is mucosal-only, causes bloody diarrhea, and carries increased colon cancer risk.

cards.front: How do internal and external hemorrhoids differ in symptoms and innervation relative to the dentate line?
cards.back: Internal hemorrhoids lie above the dentate line, have visceral innervation, and cause painless bleeding. External hemorrhoids lie below the dentate line, have somatic innervation, and are painful, so their management differs accordingly.

sections.title: GI hormones and what controls their release

cards.front: What single distinction separates a true GI hormone from a paracrine signal, and which gastric molecule is the classic paracrine example?
cards.back: Hormones travel through the bloodstream to reach distant targets, whereas paracrines diffuse locally through interstitial fluid to nearby cells. Histamine in the stomach is the canonical paracrine.

cards.front: Which enteroendocrine cell produces secretin, where is it located, and what triggers its release?
cards.back: Secretin is produced by S cells in the duodenum, and it is released in response to an acidic luminal pH below 4.5.

cards.front: Which duodenal cell type secretes CCK, and what luminal nutrients stimulate it?
cards.back: CCK is secreted by duodenal I cells, and its release is triggered by fats and proteins in the duodenum.

cards.front: Which cell releases GIP, what nutrient triggers it, and what is its special hormonal nickname?
cards.back: GIP is released by duodenal K cells in response to glucose. It is the incretin, meaning it potentiates insulin release after oral glucose.

cards.front: Which enteroendocrine cell secretes motilin, and during what state does it act?
cards.back: Motilin is secreted by duodenal M cells. It acts between meals, driving the migrating motor complex (MMC) waves during fasting.

cards.front: Where are the G cells that produce gastrin located, and what three stimuli trigger gastrin release?
cards.back: Gastrin is produced by G cells in the gastric antrum. Its release is triggered by luminal peptides, gastric distention, and vagal gastrin-releasing peptide (GRP).

cards.front: Which gastric cell secretes somatostatin, and what is its overarching role among GI hormones?
cards.back: Somatostatin is secreted by gastric D cells. It acts as the universal inhibitor of the GI tract, suppressing secretion of most other gut hormones and gastric acid.

cards.front: Which gastric cell releases histamine, and is histamine technically a hormone or a paracrine signal?
cards.back: Histamine is released by gastric enterochromaffin-like (ECL) cells. It is a paracrine signal, not a true hormone, although it is grouped with the GI hormones for exam purposes.

cards.front: What luminal change triggers secretin, what cells does it target, and what is the net effect?
cards.back: A duodenal pH below 4.5 triggers secretin release. Secretin targets pancreatic duct cells, stimulating a bicarbonate-rich secretion that neutralizes gastric acid entering the duodenum.

cards.front: When secretin acts on the pancreas, do the duct cells secrete enzymes or bicarbonate, and which hormone drives the other product?
cards.back: Pancreatic duct cells secrete bicarbonate, not enzymes, in response to secretin. Enzyme secretion is the job of acinar cells, which are stimulated by CCK.

cards.front: A pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor causes watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, and achlorhydria. What hormone is overproduced and what is the syndrome called?
cards.back: Excess VIP causes WDHA syndrome (watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria), also called Verner-Morrison syndrome or pancreatic cholera, from a VIPoma.

cards.front: What laboratory test confirms a suspected VIPoma, and why is chromogranin A not sufficient?
cards.back: A VIPoma is confirmed by measuring an elevated serum VIP level. Chromogranin A is only a non-specific neuroendocrine marker and cannot confirm the diagnosis.

cards.front: Trace GIP from its source cell to its trigger to its ultimate target and effect.
cards.back: GIP comes from duodenal K cells and is triggered by glucose. It acts on pancreatic beta cells to stimulate insulin release, which is why it is called the glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide.

cards.front: Guanylin and uroguanylin act on which receptor, through what second messenger, and what two clinically important agents share that target?
cards.back: They act on guanylyl cyclase C, raising cGMP to drive chloride and water secretion into the lumen. The same target is hit by E. coli heat-stable (ST) toxin in traveler's diarrhea and by linaclotide, a drug for IBS-C.

cards.front: What three stimuli increase gastrin release, and what feedback signal shuts gastrin off once the antrum becomes acidic?
cards.back: Gastrin is stimulated by luminal peptides and amino acids, gastric distention, and vagal GRP. Acid in the antrum inhibits gastrin release by triggering somatostatin from D cells.

cards.front: Through which receptor on parietal cells does gastrin stimulate acid secretion, and how does it relate to the CCK receptor family?
cards.back: Gastrin acts on parietal cells via the CCK-B (CCK2) receptor. It belongs to the same receptor family as CCK, but the B subtype is the gastrin-preferring one.

cards.front: What clinical triad suggests Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, and what confirmatory test shows a paradoxical response?
cards.back: Recurrent ulcers in atypical locations, failure to respond to PPIs, and a serum gastrin above 1000 pg/mL suggest gastrinoma. The secretin stimulation test confirms it, because secretin paradoxically raises gastrin in Zollinger-Ellison syndrome.

cards.front: Roughly what fraction of gastrinomas occur in MEN1, and which three organs and screening labs does MEN1 involve?
cards.back: About 25% of gastrinomas occur as part of MEN1, which features parathyroid, pancreatic, and pituitary tumors. Screen with PTH and calcium, and prolactin.

cards.front: What three actions does CCK perform after a fatty meal, and what is the mnemonic that captures them?
cards.back: CCK contracts the gallbladder, relaxes (opens) the sphincter of Oddi, and stimulates pancreatic enzyme release. The mnemonic is Contract gallbladder, Open sphincter of Oddi, K-ick the pancreas.

cards.front: Which luminal nutrients trigger CCK, and which nutrients trigger GIP and secretin instead?
cards.back: CCK is triggered by fats and proteins in the duodenum. Carbohydrates (glucose) trigger GIP, and luminal acid triggers secretin.

cards.front: Where are the CCK-A and CCK-B receptors located, and which one is actually the gastrin receptor?
cards.back: CCK-A (CCK1) receptors are on the gallbladder, pancreatic acinar cells, and brainstem satiety centers. CCK-B (CCK2) receptors are on parietal cells and serve as the gastrin receptor.

cards.front: On a CCK-stimulated HIDA scan, what gallbladder ejection fraction is normal, and what value suggests biliary dyskinesia?
cards.back: In a normal patient CCK contracts the gallbladder to an ejection fraction above 35%. An ejection fraction below 35% suggests biliary dyskinesia.

cards.front: On the parietal cell, what receptor does histamine use, what G-protein and second-messenger cascade does it activate, and why is it called the amplifier?
cards.back: Histamine binds the H2 receptor, a Gs-coupled GPCR that activates adenylyl cyclase to raise cAMP and activate PKA. It comes from ECL cells as a paracrine and amplifies acid secretion, so blocking it weakens the gastrin and ACh pathways too.

cards.front: Which parietal cell receptor does gastrin use, and what G-protein and signaling pathway does it engage?
cards.back: Gastrin binds the CCK-B (CCK2) receptor, a Gq-coupled GPCR that activates phospholipase C, producing IP3 and DAG and raising intracellular calcium. Gastrin is a true endocrine hormone from G cells.

cards.front: Which muscarinic receptor mediates ACh stimulation of parietal cells, what pathway does it use, and what is its source?
cards.back: ACh acts on the M3 muscarinic receptor, a Gq-coupled GPCR that activates phospholipase C to generate IP3 and DAG and raise intracellular calcium. The ACh comes from the vagus nerve as a neurocrine signal.

cards.front: Regardless of which of the three receptors is stimulated, what pump is the final common pathway for acid secretion, and what does it exchange?
cards.back: The H+/K+ ATPase is the final common pathway. It pumps hydrogen ions out of the parietal cell into the lumen in exchange for potassium ions moving in.

cards.front: Why are PPIs prodrugs, and what makes their inhibition of the proton pump so durable?
cards.back: PPIs (the -prazoles) are prodrugs activated by gastric acid, after which they bind irreversibly via a disulfide bond to a cysteine on the H+/K+ ATPase. The pump enzyme must be newly synthesized for acid secretion to resume, giving a 12 to 24 hour duration of effect.

cards.front: What enzyme supplies the hydrogen ions for parietal cell acid secretion, and what class of drug modestly reduces acid as a side effect by inhibiting it?
cards.back: Carbonic anhydrase generates the hydrogen ions used for HCl secretion. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors such as acetazolamide modestly reduce gastric acid as a side effect, though they are not first-line acid therapy.

cards.front: What is the alkaline tide, what causes it, and is it clinically symptomatic?
cards.back: The alkaline tide is a transient postprandial rise in venous bicarbonate caused by basolateral export of HCO3- as the parietal cell secretes acid. It is mild, transient, and asymptomatic, but a high-yield physiology concept.

cards.front: Destruction of parietal cells produces pernicious anemia. What is the triad that results, and why does each component occur?
cards.back: Pernicious anemia gives B12 deficiency with megaloblastic anemia and neurologic symptoms, achlorhydria, and secondary hypergastrinemia. All three follow from loss of parietal cells, which normally make intrinsic factor and acid.

cards.front: By what mechanism do NSAIDs cause peptic ulcers, and how do PPIs versus misoprostol counter that mechanism?
cards.back: NSAIDs inhibit COX, reducing PGE2 and thereby removing both mucosal protection and prostaglandin-mediated acid suppression. PPIs counter only the acid arm, whereas misoprostol, a PGE1 analog, restores both mucosal protection and acid suppression.

cards.front: How do antacids reduce gastric acidity at the molecular level, and what are two common antacid formulations?
cards.back: Antacids work by direct chemical neutralization of acid in the lumen, with no receptor or pump targeting. Tums is calcium carbonate, and Maalox combines aluminum hydroxide and magnesium hydroxide.

cards.front: Match each antacid cation to its characteristic side effect: aluminum, magnesium, calcium, and sodium bicarbonate.
cards.back: Aluminum causes constipation and magnesium causes diarrhea, so they are combined to balance bowel effects. Calcium can cause milk-alkali syndrome at high doses, and sodium bicarbonate causes metabolic alkalosis with a sodium load.

cards.front: By blocking the H2 receptor, how do H2 blockers indirectly weaken all three acid-stimulating pathways, and how do they compare to PPIs?
cards.back: H2 blockers remove the cAMP amplifier that histamine provides, indirectly weakening the gastrin and ACh pathways as well. They are less powerful than PPIs but have a faster onset.

cards.front: Why has cimetidine largely been replaced by famotidine? Name its hormonal and drug-interaction side effects.
cards.back: Cimetidine has anti-androgen effects causing gynecomastia, galactorrhea, and impotence, and it is a potent CYP450 inhibitor with many interactions, including warfarin, phenytoin, and theophylline. Famotidine is cleaner and preferred.

cards.front: What exactly do PPIs inhibit, by what type of bond, and why does this give maximal acid suppression?
cards.back: PPIs irreversibly and covalently inhibit the H+/K+ ATPase. Because this pump is the final common pathway for acid secretion, blocking it produces maximal acid suppression regardless of the stimulus.

cards.front: List the major clinical indications for proton pump inhibitors.
cards.back: PPIs are used for chronic GERD, erosive esophagitis, and peptic ulcer disease, for Zollinger-Ellison syndrome at high doses, for prevention of NSAID ulcers, and for stress ulcer prophylaxis in ICU patients.

cards.front: What are the recognized long-term risks of PPI therapy captured by the BAD MICE mnemonic?
cards.back: Long-term PPI risks include B12 deficiency, bone loss/osteoporosis, acquired community-acquired pneumonia, C. difficile infection, low magnesium, interstitial nephritis with CKD, and acid rebound on discontinuation.

cards.front: How are microsporidia now classified, and what key structural features define these organisms that cause GI infection in immunocompromised patients?
cards.back: Microsporidia are now classified as fungi (reclassified from protozoa). They are obligate intracellular, spore-forming organisms with small spores roughly 1 to 2 micrometers in size.

cards.front: Which patient populations are at greatest risk for symptomatic microsporidiosis, and what happens when an immunocompetent person is infected?
cards.back: The main risk factor is immunocompromise, especially HIV with a CD4 count below 100 or organ transplant. Infections do occur in immunocompetent people but are rare and self-limited.

cards.front: How is microsporidiosis transmitted, and what makes its spores environmentally hardy?
cards.back: Transmission is fecal-oral via contaminated water or food. The spores are durable and can survive in the environment for months.

cards.front: By what stepwise mechanism does a microsporidian spore invade an enterocyte and ultimately produce diarrhea?
cards.back: The spore everts its polar filament to directly inject sporoplasm into the host cell cytoplasm, where it replicates intracellularly. This leads to enterocyte rupture and villous atrophy, producing diarrhea.

cards.front: What is the classic clinical presentation of GI microsporidiosis in an HIV patient?
cards.back: An HIV patient with a low CD4 count presenting with chronic non-bloody watery diarrhea, weight loss, and fat malabsorption.

cards.front: Which stain is used to diagnose microsporidiosis, and what does a positive result look like under the microscope?
cards.back: The modified trichrome (Weber) stain is used. It shows pink-red spores against a green background.

cards.front: How does treatment of intestinal microsporidiosis differ by species, and what additional therapy is essential in HIV patients?
cards.back: Enterocytozoon bieneusi is treated with fumagillin, while Encephalitozoon intestinalis is treated with albendazole. In both cases, antiretroviral therapy is given to restore immune function.

cards.front: Among fungal intoxications, what is the clinical significance of mycetismus gastrointestinalis and how much exam attention does it warrant?
cards.back: Mycetismus gastrointestinalis is a benign, self-limited mushroom intoxication. It is low-yield and not an exam focus, so it is enough to simply recognize the term.

cards.front: How does the time of symptom onset help distinguish benign mushroom intoxication from lethal amatoxin poisoning?
cards.back: Early onset under 6 hours with a self-limited course points to benign mycetismus. Delayed onset at 6 to 24 hours suggests amatoxin poisoning, which is potentially lethal.

cards.front: What is the molecular mechanism of alpha-amanitin toxicity, and which organs bear the brunt of the damage?
cards.back: Alpha-amanitin irreversibly inhibits RNA polymerase II, halting mRNA synthesis so no new protein is made and the cell dies. Hepatocytes are hit hardest because they are highly transcription-dependent, with renal proximal tubule cells affected second.

cards.front: For amatoxin poisoning, what aspect of the toxin should you focus on rather than its chemical structure?
cards.back: Focus on the mechanism, which is inhibition of RNA polymerase II. The detailed chemical structure of the toxin is not a testable point.

cards.front: What characterizes phase 1 of amatoxin (amanitin) poisoning, and why can it be misleading?
cards.back: Phase 1, roughly 6 to 24 hours after ingestion, presents with nausea, severe vomiting, watery or bloody diarrhea, and abdominal cramps. It mimics ordinary food poisoning, which can be misleading.

cards.front: What happens during phase 2 of amatoxin poisoning, and why is this stage clinically dangerous?
cards.back: Phase 2, about 24 to 48 hours in, brings apparent clinical improvement as GI symptoms abate. It is dangerous because patients may be falsely reassured or discharged just before liver failure sets in.

cards.front: What defines phase 3 of amatoxin poisoning, and which lab and clinical findings appear?
cards.back: Phase 3, around days 3 to 5, is fulminant hepatic failure with ALT and AST climbing into the thousands, rising INR, jaundice, and hepatic encephalopathy. It is often accompanied by acute kidney injury.

cards.front: What are the immediate management steps for suspected amatoxin mushroom poisoning, and what must you avoid doing?
cards.back: Give activated charcoal if within 6 hours, provide aggressive IV fluids, identify the mushroom, and transfer to a liver center. Do not discharge the patient based on the apparent improvement of phase 2.

cards.front: Which fungi produce aflatoxin, and under what storage conditions and foods does contamination typically occur?
cards.back: Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus produce aflatoxin. Contamination occurs on improperly stored grains such as corn, peanuts, nuts, and oilseeds.

cards.front: For aflatoxin, which two facts should you prioritize over memorizing its isoforms?
cards.back: Prioritize the mechanism of toxicity and its association with hepatocellular carcinoma. The specific isoforms (B1, B2, G1, G2) are not testable.

cards.front: By what mechanism does aflatoxin cause hepatocellular carcinoma at the molecular level?
cards.back: CYP450 activates aflatoxin into a reactive epoxide that forms a DNA adduct. This produces a characteristic p53 codon 249 mutation, driving hepatocellular carcinoma.

cards.front: Why is the combination of hepatitis B and aflatoxin exposure especially dangerous for hepatocellular carcinoma risk?
cards.back: HBV alone and aflatoxin alone each raise HCC risk only modestly, but together their effects synergize. The combined risk multiplies to roughly 30 to 60 times baseline.

cards.front: How is aflatoxin contamination prevented, and why does cooking fail to make contaminated grain safe?
cards.back: Prevention relies on keeping grains dry and cool to prevent fungal growth. Cooking does not help because aflatoxins are heat-stable.

cards.front: What is coprine, where does it come from, and what is its active metabolite?
cards.back: Coprine is a toxin that acts like Antabuse (disulfiram) and comes from the mushroom Coprinus atramentarius. Its active metabolite is 1-aminocyclopropanol.

cards.front: How does the coprine reaction produce its symptoms when a person drinks alcohol after eating the mushroom?
cards.back: The coprine metabolite blocks aldehyde dehydrogenase, just like disulfiram. Acetaldehyde then accumulates when alcohol is consumed, causing flushing, tachycardia, and nausea.

cards.front: What counseling should a patient receive after eating Coprinus atramentarius regarding alcohol?
cards.back: Advise avoiding alcohol for about 3 days after eating Coprinus atramentarius. Drinking alcohol outside that window produces no symptoms.

cards.front: What is the timing of onset and resolution of the coprine-alcohol (Antabuse-like) reaction?
cards.back: Symptoms begin 15 to 30 minutes after drinking alcohol. The reaction is self-limited and resolves within 2 to 3 hours.

cards.front: How is the coprine-alcohol reaction treated, and is a specific antidote available?
cards.back: Treatment is supportive with fluids, anti-emetics, and monitoring. There is no specific antidote, and the reaction is self-limited.

sections.title: Digestion, absorption, and secretion across the gut

cards.front: Why is the small intestine, not the colon, built for nutrient absorption, and what does that structural choice cost the colon?
cards.back: The small intestine has villi and microvilli that create an enormous surface area optimized for nutrient absorption, while the colon lacks villi. The trade-off is that the colon cannot absorb nutrients and is instead specialized to dehydrate chyme by reabsorbing water and electrolytes.

cards.front: In the small intestine versus the colon, where are villi found and how does that match each segment's primary job?
cards.back: Villi are present only in the small intestine, where their job is nutrient absorption. The colon has no villi because its job is to dehydrate chyme by reabsorbing water rather than absorb nutrients.

cards.front: Among the two enterocyte populations of the intestinal epithelium, which one absorbs and which one secretes?
cards.back: Absorptive enterocytes on the villi carry out absorption, while crypt enterocytes carry out secretion. These are their primary respective roles.

cards.front: After a fatty meal, which single hormone simultaneously empties the gallbladder, opens the sphincter of Oddi, and revs the pancreas, and why does it reappear in the motility lecture?
cards.back: Cholecystokinin (CCK), released by duodenal I cells in response to fat and protein, contracts the gallbladder, relaxes the sphincter of Oddi, and stimulates pancreatic enzyme secretion. It reappears in the motility lecture because it also inhibits gastric emptying and promotes orad gastric accommodation.

cards.front: Which cells release CCK, what triggers it, and what are its three actions on the gallbladder, sphincter of Oddi, and pancreas?
cards.back: CCK is released by duodenal I cells in response to fat and protein. It contracts the gallbladder, relaxes the sphincter of Oddi, and stimulates pancreatic secretion and growth.

cards.front: Beyond its digestive actions, what effect does CCK have on gastric emptying, and why does that matter for the motility lectures?
cards.back: CCK is a potent inhibitor of gastric emptying. This is a motility action that carries forward into the motility lectures (Lec 5 to 6).

cards.front: What fraction of bile acids does the terminal ileum recycle, by what mechanism, and what vitamin does that same segment absorb?
cards.back: The terminal ileum recycles about 95% of bile acids via the enterohepatic circulation, returning them to the liver. The same segment also absorbs vitamin B12.

cards.front: For each macronutrient, what are the rate-limiting enzyme, apical transporter, and exit door, and which single transporter do all three monosaccharides share on the way out?
cards.back: Carbohydrates use brush-border disaccharidases, with glucose and galactose entering via apical SGLT1 and fructose via GLUT5; protein relies on enterokinase-activated trypsin and apical PepT1/amino-acid transporters; lipids depend on pancreatic lipase with micellar uptake. All three monosaccharides exit the basolateral membrane through GLUT2.

cards.front: What distinguishes the SGLT1 and SGLT2 transporters in terms of location and the sugars they move?
cards.back: SGLT1 is the apical, sodium-coupled transporter that absorbs glucose and galactose in the intestine. SGLT2 is found in the kidney, and the SGLT1 mechanism is the basis for oral rehydration solution.

cards.front: Which transporter handles apical fructose uptake, which provides the basolateral exit for all three monosaccharides, and what is the classic trap?
cards.back: GLUT5 mediates apical, sodium-independent fructose absorption, while GLUT2 is the basolateral exit for all three monosaccharides. The classic trap is mistakenly placing GLUT2 on the apical membrane.

cards.front: Why does lactase deficiency cause an osmotic diarrhea while maltose digestion is comparatively protected?
cards.back: Lactose can only be digested by lactase, so its deficiency leaves undigested lactose in the lumen that draws in water and causes osmotic diarrhea. Maltose is protected because it has redundant enzymes that can digest it.

cards.front: How is trypsinogen activated in the duodenum, and why is enterokinase the key to the entire protease cascade?
cards.back: The brush-border enzyme enterokinase, found only in the duodenum, activates trypsinogen to trypsin. Trypsin then activates all the other pancreatic proteases, making enterokinase the trigger for the whole cascade.

cards.front: In cystinuria and Hartnup disease, which amino acids are affected and how can the body still rescue absorption despite a defective amino-acid transporter?
cards.back: Cystinuria affects the cationic amino acids (cystine, ornithine, lysine, arginine; COLA) and Hartnup disease affects neutral amino acids such as tryptophan. Both are rescued because dipeptides can still be absorbed via PepT1, though Hartnup can lead to pellagra from tryptophan loss.

cards.front: In lipid digestion, which enzyme is essential, what role do bile acids play, and how do lipid products actually reach the enterocyte?
cards.back: Pancreatic lipase is the essential enzyme for fat digestion, while bile acids emulsify fat to increase surface area. The lipid breakdown products are then ferried to the enterocyte inside micelles, with lingual and gastric lipases playing only minor roles.

cards.front: After absorption, how are triglycerides repackaged and routed out of the enterocyte, and what is the exception that uses the portal route instead?
cards.back: Re-synthesized triglycerides are packaged into chylomicrons that enter the lacteals and travel via lymph rather than the portal vein. The exception is medium-chain fatty acids, which are absorbed directly into the portal circulation.

cards.front: The gut handles about 8.5 L of fluid a day but loses only ~100 mL, so where does all that fluid come from, and why is the jejunum, not the duodenum, the sodium workhorse?
cards.back: Of the roughly 8.5 L presented daily, about 6.5 L is endogenous GI secretion and only about 2 L is dietary, with only ~100 mL lost in stool. The jejunum is the major site of sodium absorption because of its high absorptive capacity, whereas the duodenum specializes more in nutrient and iron handling.

cards.front: Of the ~8.5 L of fluid presented to the gut each day, how much comes from secretion versus diet, how much reaches the colon, and how much is lost?
cards.back: About 8.5 L is presented per day and only roughly 100 mL is lost in stool. Approximately 6.5 L is GI secretion and about 2 L is dietary, with around 2 L reaching the colon.

cards.front: What are the main intestinal sodium absorption mechanisms, what feature do they share, and what ultimately powers them all?
cards.back: Sodium is absorbed via ENaC (colon, aldosterone-regulated), sodium-nutrient cotransport (duodenum), sodium-chloride cotransport, and the sodium-hydrogen exchanger (NHE), all of which are electroneutral. The basolateral Na/K-ATPase powers all of these by maintaining the sodium gradient.

cards.front: How do the duodenum, jejunum, and ileum divide the labor of absorption?
cards.back: The duodenum specializes in nutrients and iron, the jejunum is the major site of sodium absorption, and the ileum handles vitamin B12 and bile acid recycling. This reflects segment-by-segment specialization of the small intestine.

cards.front: How does the route of intestinal calcium absorption shift between high and low intake, and which vitamin governs the regulated pathway?
cards.back: With high calcium intake, absorption is mainly paracellular, while with low intake it becomes transcellular through an apical channel, calbindin, and a basolateral Ca-ATPase. The transcellular pathway is regulated by vitamin D3.

cards.front: Walk through the steps of non-heme iron absorption in the duodenum, from luminal reduction to fate inside the enterocyte.
cards.back: Dietary ferric iron (Fe3+) is reduced to ferrous iron (Fe2+) by the brush-border reductase DCYTB, then taken up across the apical membrane by DMT1. Inside the enterocyte iron is either stored as ferritin or exported into the blood through ferroportin (FPN1).

cards.front: Intestinal secretion uses the same chloride channel whether the trigger is cAMP or calcium, so how does cholera toxin turn a normal physiologic process into life-threatening diarrhea?
cards.back: Both cAMP and calcium signaling open the apical CFTR chloride channel, after which sodium and water follow into the lumen. Cholera toxin permanently elevates cAMP so CFTR stays open continuously, driving massive, unregulated secretory diarrhea that persists even when the patient stops eating.

cards.front: What is the shared final pathway by which both cAMP and calcium signals drive intestinal fluid secretion?
cards.back: Both cAMP and calcium open the apical chloride channel CFTR, allowing chloride to enter the lumen. Sodium then follows for electroneutrality and water follows osmotically, producing secretion through a single common endpoint.

cards.front: How do the secretory triggers sort into cAMP-mediated versus calcium-mediated messengers?
cards.back: cAMP-mediated triggers include cholera toxin, E. coli toxin, VIP, histamine, and prostaglandin. Calcium-mediated triggers include acetylcholine, bradykinin, and serotonin.

cards.front: Why does the diarrhea caused by cholera or E. coli toxin continue even when the patient is kept NPO?
cards.back: Cholera and E. coli toxins permanently raise intracellular cAMP, so the CFTR chloride channel never closes. This drives continuous secretory diarrhea that persists even when the patient stops eating.

cards.front: Across the four mechanisms of diarrhea, which type keeps going when the patient stops eating, and which lab pattern unmasks a destroyed villous surface?
cards.back: Secretory diarrhea (from toxins or VIP) persists with fasting, whereas osmotic diarrhea (such as lactose intolerance) stops with fasting; the other two mechanisms are inflammatory and dysmotility. A destroyed villous surface, as in celiac disease, is unmasked by a low d-xylose absorption test along with steatorrhea and signs of pan-malabsorption.

cards.front: What is the fasting test, and how does it separate secretory from osmotic diarrhea?
cards.back: The fasting test distinguishes diarrhea by whether it stops when the patient stops eating. Secretory diarrhea (toxin or VIP driven) persists with fasting, while osmotic diarrhea (such as lactose intolerance) stops with fasting.

cards.front: Beyond secretory and osmotic, what are the underlying defects in inflammatory and dysmotility diarrhea?
cards.back: Inflammatory diarrhea results from loss of absorptive surface area, while dysmotility diarrhea results from transit that is too fast for adequate absorption. These complete the four-mechanism framework of diarrhea.

cards.front: What constellation of findings makes celiac disease a pan-malabsorptive process rather than an isolated deficiency?
cards.back: Celiac disease causes pan-malabsorption from loss of absorptive surface area, producing steatorrhea, a low d-xylose absorption test, and hypoalbuminemia with edema. The d-xylose abnormality points specifically to a mucosal surface-area defect.

cards.front: Which vitamins are lost in steatorrhea, and why is B12 deficiency a different problem altogether?
cards.back: Steatorrhea causes loss of the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K. Vitamin B12 deficiency is instead an ileum or intrinsic-factor problem, and the water-soluble vitamins are generally spared by fat malabsorption.

sections.title: Functional subdivisions and motility of the GI tract

cards.front: If peristalsis is the one pattern that actually moves food forward, what do segmentation, mass movement, and haustration each add that peristalsis alone cannot?
cards.back: Peristalsis propels the bolus by contracting behind and relaxing ahead. Segmentation does not propel; it mixes chyme with secretions and maximizes mucosal contact for absorption. Mass movement is the strong colonic propulsive surge that drives stool toward the rectum, and haustration is the colon's segmenting pattern that slowly shuffles contents to aid water reabsorption.

cards.front: For each of the three core motility patterns (peristalsis, segmentation, mass movement), what is its specific job and where does it dominate?
cards.back: Peristalsis is the dominant propulsive pattern, contracting behind the bolus and relaxing ahead to move it forward. Segmentation is non-propulsive mixing of chyme, and mass movement is the colon's propulsive surge that pushes contents toward the rectum.

cards.front: What is haustration, and which segment of the GI tract is it specific to?
cards.back: Haustration is the colon-specific variant of peristalsis, named for the haustra (the sacculated pockets of the colon). It produces slow, segmenting contractions that mix contents and promote water reabsorption.

cards.front: Trace a single swallow from its voluntary start to its involuntary phase, and explain why a solid meal leaves the stomach with a lag phase that a glass of water does not.
cards.back: Swallowing begins voluntarily with the oral preparatory and oral stages; once the bolus triggers the pharyngeal swallow it becomes an involuntary, brainstem-mediated reflex through the pharynx and esophagus. Liquids empty from the stomach almost immediately, but solids must first be ground by the antrum down to under about 2 mm before the pylorus lets them pass, producing the characteristic solid-meal lag phase.

cards.front: During swallowing, which stages are under voluntary control and at exactly what point does the process become involuntary?
cards.back: The oral preparatory and oral stages are voluntary. Control switches to involuntary at the pharyngeal swallow, triggered when the bolus reaches the back of the mouth/oropharynx, after which the reflex proceeds automatically.

cards.front: How is the esophagus divided by muscle type, and which neuromuscular disease characteristically affects each portion?
cards.back: The upper one-third of the esophagus is striated (skeletal) muscle and is affected by myasthenia gravis, while the lower two-thirds is smooth muscle and is affected by scleroderma. The disease maps to the muscle type involved.

cards.front: What distinguishes primary from secondary esophageal peristalsis, and which one explains the sensation of food stuck in the chest?
cards.back: Primary peristalsis is initiated by a swallow and carries the bolus down the esophagus. Secondary peristalsis is an enteric nervous system mop-up wave triggered by retained residue or distension (not by a swallow), and it is what clears food that feels stuck in the chest.

cards.front: How does the motility pattern of the stomach differ across its three functional regions (fundus/orad, corpus, antrum)?
cards.back: The orad stomach/fundus performs receptive relaxation and accommodation with sustained tonic, near-threshold activity. The corpus (body) provides mixing, and the antrum generates the strong contractions responsible for grinding and gastric emptying. Region determines pattern.

cards.front: What is the 2-mm rule of gastric emptying, and how does it produce the solid-meal lag phase?
cards.back: The pylorus only lets particles smaller than about 2 mm pass into the duodenum, so larger solids are retropelled for antral grinding until they are small enough. This grinding requirement creates the solid-meal lag phase, whereas liquids pass freely and show no lag.

cards.front: If slow waves never trigger a contraction by themselves, what is their actual purpose, and what converts a slow wave into a real contraction?
cards.back: Slow waves are subthreshold oscillations of the resting membrane potential generated by the interstitial cells of Cajal; they set the rhythm and timing (the maximum possible frequency) of contractions but do not cause them. A contraction occurs only when an additional stimulus, such as acetylcholine, depolarizes the slow-wave crest past threshold to fire spike potentials (action potentials).

cards.front: What exactly are gastric slow waves electrically, and are they influenced by nerves or hormones?
cards.back: Slow waves are regular, subthreshold oscillations of the membrane potential, not action potentials. Their intrinsic rhythm is generated by the interstitial cells of Cajal and is not altered in frequency by neural or hormonal input.

cards.front: What is a spike potential in GI smooth muscle, at roughly what threshold does it occur, and what makes the cell more likely to reach it?
cards.back: A spike potential is a true action potential that fires at the crest of a slow wave once the membrane reaches threshold (around -40 mV). Acetylcholine sensitizes the muscle by depolarizing it toward threshold, making spike potentials and contraction more likely.

cards.front: Which cells generate the slow waves, and what is the minimum functional unit required for a GI contraction?
cards.back: Interstitial cells of Cajal (ICCs) are the pacemaker cells that generate slow waves. Contraction requires the ICC plus smooth-muscle unit working together; loss of ICCs is a key mechanism in gastroparesis.

cards.front: Why does your stomach growl when you skip a meal, and what stops that pattern the moment you eat, but only if you eat by mouth rather than receiving IV nutrition?
cards.back: During fasting the migrating motor complex (MMC) sweeps the stomach and small bowel about every 90 minutes to clear residue and bacteria, and its strong contractions produce the growling (borborygmi). The MMC is switched off by enteral nutrients physically present in the gut; intravenous feeding bypasses the lumen and therefore does not stop it.

cards.front: What is the migrating motor complex, how often does it cycle, and what happens clinically when it is reduced?
cards.back: The MMC is the fasting-only housekeeping motility pattern that sweeps the small bowel roughly every 90 minutes to clear residue and keep it sterile. When it is reduced (for example in IBS), bacterial overgrowth can occur, leading to small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO).

cards.front: What stops the migrating motor complex, and which part of the nervous system drives it?
cards.back: The MMC is halted by the physical presence of enteral nutrients in the gut, not by intravenous feeding. It is driven entirely by the enteric nervous system and does not require a vagovagal reflex.

cards.front: How do motilin and CCK have opposing effects on gastric emptying and the MMC?
cards.back: Motilin stimulates the migrating motor complex and promotes gastric emptying during fasting. CCK acts in the opposite direction by inhibiting gastric emptying after a meal.

cards.front: Vomiting uses the same muscular machinery as normal forward propulsion, so what single change converts orderly forward motility into expulsion, and what triggers it?
cards.back: The change is reversal of direction: a retrograde antiperistaltic wave runs from the small bowel up through the relaxed LES and UES to expel contents through the mouth. It is coordinated by the vomiting center in the medulla, which is activated by inputs such as the chemoreceptor trigger zone (e.g., chemotherapy, toxins), GI irritation, the vestibular system, and higher cortical centers.

cards.front: In terms of motility, what is vomiting and how does it differ directionally from normal peristalsis?
cards.back: Vomiting is a retrograde antiperistaltic wave that moves contents backward, out through the relaxed lower esophageal sphincter and upper esophageal sphincter to the mouth. It is essentially reversed peristalsis rather than a new mechanism.

cards.front: What is the role of the chemoreceptor trigger zone in emesis, and what is its classic activating stimulus?
cards.back: The chemoreceptor trigger zone (CRTZ) detects circulating toxins and drugs and signals the vomiting center to initiate emesis. Chemotherapy is the classic trigger acting through this irritant-driven pathway.

cards.front: In the smooth-muscle esophagus, why must the inhibitory signal fire before the excitatory one, and how can a single GI hormone coordinate both accommodation and emptying?
cards.back: Inhibitory neurons (releasing NO and VIP) fire first to relax the segment ahead of the bolus so it can advance, and excitatory cholinergic neurons then contract the segment behind it, creating the orderly aboral wave. CCK is the hormone that coordinates both ends of the stomach: it promotes orad receptive accommodation while simultaneously inhibiting distal gastric emptying.

cards.front: Which neurotransmitters mediate the excitatory and inhibitory limbs of esophageal peristalsis, and why must inhibition lead?
cards.back: Acetylcholine is excitatory and causes contraction, while NO and VIP are inhibitory and cause relaxation. The inhibitory signal fires first, ahead of the bolus, so the segment relaxes before the contraction behind it pushes the bolus forward; inhibitory tone predominates in the lower esophagus.

cards.front: Which GI motility functions depend on the vagovagal reflex, and which are purely enteric?
cards.back: Receptive relaxation/accommodation and pancreatic secretion are vagovagal (require the vagus). The migrating motor complex is enteric-only and runs without any vagovagal input, illustrating the split in motility control systems.

cards.front: What are the two distinct gastric actions of CCK, and why are they considered an elegant pairing?
cards.back: CCK inhibits gastric emptying while also promoting orad (fundic) accommodation. With one hormone it both relaxes the proximal stomach to hold food and slows distal emptying, coordinating storage and controlled release.

cards.front: Achalasia, gastroparesis, Hirschsprung disease, and fecal incontinence each break a different link in the motility chain. Which link does each disrupt, and what is the diagnostic tell for each?
cards.back: Achalasia is loss of inhibitory (NO/VIP) myenteric neurons so the LES fails to relax and peristalsis is lost, causing dysphagia to both solids and liquids with a bird-beak on barium swallow. Gastroparesis is impaired gastric emptying (commonly from diabetic ICC/autonomic loss), giving early satiety, bloating, and delayed emptying. Hirschsprung disease is congenital absence of ganglion cells in the distal bowel causing a tonically contracted, obstructed segment with failure to pass meconium, and fecal incontinence reflects failure of the voluntary external anal sphincter and continence mechanism.

cards.front: What is the motility defect in achalasia, and what are its classic clinical and imaging findings?
cards.back: In achalasia the LES fails to relax and esophageal peristalsis is lost due to degeneration of inhibitory myenteric neurons. This causes dysphagia to both solids and liquids and the classic bird-beak appearance of the distal esophagus on barium swallow.

cards.front: How does gastroparesis differ from the dumping that follows pylorectomy, and what underlies diabetic gastroparesis?
cards.back: Gastroparesis is disorganized, delayed gastric emptying, classically from diabetes-related loss of interstitial cells of Cajal. Pylorectomy produces the opposite extreme, dumping syndrome, where gastric contents empty into the small bowel too rapidly.

cards.front: What is the underlying defect in Hirschsprung disease, and which structure is required for voluntary continence?
cards.back: Hirschsprung disease results from failed enteric neural crest migration, leaving an aganglionic, tonically contracted distal bowel segment that causes functional obstruction. Continence depends on the voluntary external anal sphincter.

cards.front: If peristalsis is the one pattern that actually moves food forward, what do segmentation, mass movement, and haustration each add that peristalsis alone cannot?
cards.back: Peristalsis propels by contracting behind and relaxing ahead of the bolus, but segmentation mixes chyme with secretions to maximize absorption, mass movement provides the strong propulsive colonic surge that pushes contents toward the rectum, and haustration is the colon's slow churning that aids water reabsorption.

cards.front: Among the major GI motility patterns, which one is the dominant propulsive pattern, which one mixes, and which is the propulsive surge unique to the colon?
cards.back: Peristalsis is the dominant propulsive pattern (contract behind, relax ahead). Segmentation mixes contents without net forward movement. Mass movement is the colonic propulsive surge that advances stool toward the rectum.

cards.front: What is haustration, and which segment of the GI tract is it specific to?
cards.back: Haustration is the colon's variant of peristalsis, named for the haustra (the sacculated pockets of the colon). It is colon-specific and helps churn and dehydrate the fecal stream.

cards.front: Trace a single swallow from voluntary to involuntary control, and explain why a solid meal leaves the stomach with a lag phase that a glass of water does not.
cards.back: Swallowing begins voluntarily with the oral preparatory and oral stages, then becomes involuntary at the pharyngeal swallow, which triggers esophageal peristalsis. Solids show a gastric emptying lag phase because the pylorus excludes particles larger than 2 mm, so the antrum must grind them down first, whereas liquids pass through immediately with no lag.

cards.front: During swallowing, which stages are under voluntary control, and at what point does the process become involuntary?
cards.back: The oral preparatory and oral stages are voluntary. Control becomes involuntary at the pharyngeal swallow, which is triggered as the bolus reaches the back of the mouth and initiates the reflexive swallowing sequence.

cards.front: How is the esophagus divided by muscle type, and which disease characteristically affects each portion?
cards.back: The upper one-third of the esophagus is striated (skeletal) muscle, affected in myasthenia gravis, while the lower two-thirds is smooth muscle, affected in scleroderma. Disease thus maps to the muscle type involved.

cards.front: What is the difference between primary and secondary esophageal peristalsis, and what triggers each?
cards.back: Primary peristalsis is swallow-initiated and carries the bolus down the esophagus. Secondary peristalsis is an enteric nervous system mop-up wave triggered by residual food or distension, clearing material that did not fully pass and producing the sensation of food stuck in the chest.

cards.front: In the stomach, which region performs accommodation, which performs mixing, and which performs emptying?
cards.back: The orad stomach (fundus) performs accommodation through tonic, near-threshold relaxation. The corpus (body) mixes. The antrum performs grinding and emptying. Each region's motility pattern matches its function.

cards.front: Why does a solid meal produce a gastric emptying lag phase while liquids do not?
cards.back: The pylorus excludes particles larger than 2 mm, so solids must undergo antral grinding before they can pass into the duodenum, creating the solid-meal lag phase. Liquids are not size-restricted and empty without a lag.

cards.front: If slow waves never directly cause a contraction by themselves, what is their function, and what converts a slow wave into an actual contraction?
cards.back: Slow waves are rhythmic subthreshold oscillations of the membrane potential that set the pacemaking frequency and timing for possible contractions. A contraction occurs only when an excitatory input (such as ACh) brings the slow-wave crest above threshold, firing spike potentials (true action potentials) that drive smooth muscle contraction.

cards.front: What exactly are GI slow waves, and are they influenced by neural or hormonal input?
cards.back: Slow waves are regular subthreshold membrane oscillations, not action potentials, and they serve only a pacemaking role. Their basic rhythm is intrinsic and is not changed in frequency by neural or hormonal input.

cards.front: What is a spike potential in GI smooth muscle, and roughly what membrane voltage must be reached for it to fire?
cards.back: A spike potential is a true action potential that fires at the crest of a slow wave once threshold (around -40 mV) is reached. Acetylcholine sensitizes the muscle so that the slow-wave crest is more likely to reach threshold and trigger spikes.

cards.front: Which cells generate GI slow waves, and what functional unit is required for a contraction to occur?
cards.back: The interstitial cells of Cajal (ICCs) generate slow waves. Contraction requires the combined ICC and smooth-muscle unit. Loss of ICCs (as in gastroparesis) impairs the ability to generate coordinated contractions.

cards.front: Why does your stomach growl when you skip a meal, and what stops that pattern the moment you eat — but only if you eat, not if you are fed intravenously?
cards.back: The growling reflects the migrating motor complex (MMC), a fasting-only housekeeping pattern that sweeps the gut roughly every 90 minutes to clear residue and keep the small bowel sterile. It is halted by enteral nutrients physically present in the gut, so IV (parenteral) feeding does not stop it because no food contacts the bowel lumen.

cards.front: What is the migrating motor complex, how often does it occur, and what is the clinical consequence of losing it?
cards.back: The MMC is a fasting-only housekeeping pattern that occurs roughly every 90 minutes to sweep the small bowel and keep it sterile. Reduced MMC activity (as seen in IBS) predisposes to small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO).

cards.front: What stops the migrating motor complex, and which part of the nervous system drives it?
cards.back: The MMC is stopped by enteral nutrients physically present in the gut, not by intravenous feeding. It is driven entirely by the enteric nervous system without a vagovagal reflex.

cards.front: What are the opposing effects of motilin and CCK on the MMC and gastric emptying?
cards.back: Motilin stimulates the MMC and promotes gastric emptying, whereas CCK inhibits gastric emptying. They act as opposing hormones in controlling motility.

cards.front: Vomiting uses the same machinery as normal propulsion — so what single change turns orderly forward motility into expulsion, and what triggers it?
cards.back: The single change is reversal of the propulsive wave: a retrograde antiperistaltic wave moves contents backward up through the LES, UES, and out the mouth. It is initiated centrally by the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CRTZ), classically triggered by irritants such as chemotherapy agents.

cards.front: In mechanical terms, how does vomiting differ from normal peristalsis?
cards.back: Vomiting is a retrograde antiperistaltic wave that moves contents backward, out through the lower esophageal sphincter, then the upper esophageal sphincter, and finally the mouth. It is essentially reversed peristalsis.

cards.front: What structure initiates emesis, and what is the classic irritant trigger acting on it?
cards.back: The chemoreceptor trigger zone (CRTZ) initiates emesis. Chemotherapy is the classic irritant-driven trigger that stimulates the CRTZ to provoke vomiting.

cards.front: In the smooth-muscle esophagus, why must the inhibitory signal fire before the excitatory one, and how can a single GI hormone coordinate accommodation and emptying at once?
cards.back: Inhibitory neurons (NO/VIP) must fire first to relax the segment ahead of the bolus so that the following excitatory ACh-driven contraction can push the bolus into a receptive, relaxed segment, producing orderly peristalsis. CCK coordinates both ends of digestion by inhibiting gastric emptying while promoting orad (fundic) accommodation, slowing delivery to the duodenum.

cards.front: Which neurotransmitters drive excitatory versus inhibitory esophageal motility, and which fires first relative to the bolus?
cards.back: Acetylcholine is excitatory and contracts the muscle, while nitric oxide and VIP are inhibitory and relax it. Inhibition fires first, ahead of the bolus, and the lower esophagus has relatively more inhibitory tone.

cards.front: Which motility functions depend on a vagovagal reflex, and which is purely enteric?
cards.back: Gastric accommodation and pancreatic secretion are mediated by vagovagal reflexes. The migrating motor complex is enteric-only, requiring no vagovagal input. This reflects a split in the control system between CNS-linked and purely local control.

cards.front: What are the two simultaneous motility actions of CCK on the stomach?
cards.back: CCK inhibits gastric emptying while simultaneously promoting orad (fundic) accommodation. This single hormone thus slows delivery of chyme to the duodenum and helps the proximal stomach relax to store the meal.

cards.front: Achalasia, gastroparesis, Hirschsprung disease, and fecal incontinence each break a different link in the motility chain — which link does each break, and what is the tell for each?
cards.back: Achalasia breaks LES relaxation and esophageal peristalsis (dysphagia to both solids and liquids with a bird-beak barium sign). Gastroparesis breaks gastric emptying, often from diabetic ICC loss (delayed emptying, early satiety). Hirschsprung breaks the distal enteric ganglia (aganglionic segment, failure to pass meconium, obstruction). Fecal incontinence breaks voluntary external anal sphincter control (loss of continence).

cards.front: What is the motility defect in achalasia, and how does it present on swallowing and barium imaging?
cards.back: Achalasia is a neurogenic disorder in which the LES fails to relax and esophageal peristalsis is lost. It causes dysphagia to both solids and liquids and shows the classic bird-beak tapering on barium swallow.

cards.front: How does gastroparesis differ from a post-pylorectomy state at the level of gastric emptying?
cards.back: Gastroparesis is disorganized, delayed gastric emptying, classically from diabetic loss of interstitial cells of Cajal. After pylorectomy the opposite occurs: rapid uncontrolled emptying causes dumping syndrome. They represent opposite extremes of emptying.

cards.front: What is the underlying defect in Hirschsprung disease, and which structure is essential for maintaining continence?
cards.back: Hirschsprung disease is caused by an aganglionic distal segment of bowel resulting from failure of enteric neural crest cell migration, leading to a non-relaxing obstructed segment. Continence depends on the voluntary external anal sphincter.

sections.title: Lower esophageal sphincter and reflux mechanism
Diagnosis and the role of endoscopy

cards.front: In pure pressure terms, what condition must exist at the lower esophageal sphincter for gastric acid to reflux back up into the esophagus?
cards.back: Reflux occurs when the LES is incompetent and intragastric pressure exceeds esophageal pressure, so the pressure gradient drives stomach contents upward. A competent LES normally maintains a high-pressure zone that prevents this retrograde flow.

cards.front: What is the normal resting pressure of the lower esophageal sphincter, and below what value is it considered incompetent and prone to reflux?
cards.back: Normal LES baseline tone is about 10 to 35 mmHg. An LES resting pressure below 10 mmHg is considered incompetent and predisposes to gastroesophageal reflux.

cards.front: What two conditions must both be present for gastroesophageal reflux to occur, framed as a pressure relationship?
cards.back: Reflux requires an incompetent LES together with intragastric pressure that exceeds esophageal pressure. The resulting pressure gradient is what physically drives gastric contents back into the esophagus.

cards.front: Beyond the LES itself, what second anatomic structure forms the anti-reflux barrier at the gastroesophageal junction?
cards.back: The anti-reflux barrier is made up of the LES plus the crural diaphragm, which acts as an external sphincter. Together these two structures reinforce the high-pressure zone that prevents reflux.

cards.front: How does the prevalence of hiatal hernia differ between non-erosive reflux and severe esophagitis, and what mucosal pH drives the injury?
cards.back: Hiatal hernia is found in roughly one quarter of non-erosive reflux cases but in about three quarters of severe esophagitis cases, linking hernia to disease severity. Esophageal mucosal erosion occurs when refluxed acid lowers the pH below 4.

cards.front: Two patients with reflux are both on prescription PPIs, but one is controlled and one is not. Which GERD stage applies to each, and why does that distinction change management?
cards.back: The controlled patient on daily prescription therapy is Stage 2, whereas the patient who remains uncontrolled on prescription therapy with highly erosive disease is Stage 3. The distinction matters because failure of response on prescription PPIs signals escalation, such as endoscopy and consideration of surgery, rather than continued empiric medical management.

cards.front: How are Stage 1 and Stage 2 GERD distinguished by symptom frequency and treatment approach?
cards.back: Stage 1 GERD occurs about 1 to 2 times per month and is managed with lifestyle changes and over-the-counter agents. Stage 2 involves daily symptoms requiring prescription therapy, and the disease is controlled on that treatment.

cards.front: What defines Stage 3 versus Stage 4 GERD, and what is the hinge that separates them from earlier stages?
cards.back: Stage 3 is reflux that remains uncontrolled despite prescription therapy and is highly erosive, while Stage 4 is reflux-induced carcinoma. The hinge between the milder and more severe stages is loss of control on treatment, which marks progression toward erosive and malignant disease.

cards.front: Most GERD presents as postprandial heartburn, yet a patient with a months-long cough and no lung disease turns out to have reflux. How can GERD present atypically and escape recognition?
cards.back: GERD can present with extraesophageal or atypical symptoms such as chronic cough, hoarseness, asthma, and throat clearing rather than classic heartburn. Because these symptoms mimic pulmonary or ENT disease, reflux is easily overlooked, and clinical diagnosis on symptoms alone is only about 65% accurate.

cards.front: What are the classic typical symptoms of GERD, and what timing pattern is the giveaway?
cards.back: Typical GERD presents with heartburn, regurgitation, and a sour or bitter taste. The telling pattern is that symptoms occur about 30 to 60 minutes after meals and worsen when reclining.

cards.front: What atypical or extraesophageal symptoms can GERD produce, and how reliable is a symptom-based clinical diagnosis?
cards.back: Atypical or extraesophageal GERD can present as chronic cough, hoarseness, asthma, and throat clearing. Diagnosing GERD on clinical symptoms alone is only about 65% accurate, so these disguised presentations are easily missed.

cards.front: Why might Helicobacter pylori infection paradoxically lower GERD risk, and what is the downside of eradicating it?
cards.back: H. pylori decreases GERD risk because it reduces gastric acid production, but it raises the risk of MALT lymphoma and gastric cancer. Eradicating the infection can restore acid output and worsen GERD symptoms.

cards.front: If uncomplicated GERD requires no diagnostic testing, what specific findings should flip a patient from empiric treatment to immediate endoscopy?
cards.back: Alarm features mandate prompt endoscopy, including dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, and iron-deficiency anemia. These signs raise concern for complications such as stricture, erosive esophagitis, Barrett esophagus, or malignancy, which empiric acid suppression alone would not address.

cards.front: How is uncomplicated GERD diagnosed and managed initially, and how good is response to therapy as a diagnostic test?
cards.back: Uncomplicated GERD is managed with empiric acid suppression and no further testing. Symptom response to therapy is about 78% sensitive but only 54% specific, so it supports the diagnosis but is far from definitive.

cards.front: Which alarm features in a patient with reflux symptoms should prompt endoscopy rather than empiric treatment?
cards.back: The alarm features are dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, and iron-deficiency anemia. Any of these warrants endoscopy to evaluate for complications such as stricture, severe esophagitis, Barrett esophagus, or cancer.

cards.front: Why should endoscopy be performed before starting acid suppression when esophagitis is suspected?
cards.back: Acid-suppressing therapy heals the mucosa and can obscure underlying esophagitis, making it harder to detect on endoscopy. Scoping before treatment preserves the diagnostic findings so the true extent of disease can be assessed.

cards.front: What is a fundoplication, how does it work mechanically, and how effective is it at healing esophagitis?
cards.back: Fundoplication is a surgical procedure that wraps the gastric fundus 360 degrees around the distal esophagus to recreate and reinforce the LES. It heals esophagitis in more than 85% of patients and serves as the definitive antireflux surgical option.

cards.front: Across nearly all bacterial gastroenteritis, what is the default management approach, and which organism makes the instinct to reach for antibiotics actively harmful?
cards.back: The default for almost all bacterial gastroenteritis is supportive fluid and electrolyte repletion, since most cases are self-limited. The key trap is enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC), where antibiotics provide no benefit and increase the risk of hemolytic uremic syndrome.

cards.front: What is the default first-line management for most cases of bacterial gastroenteritis, and why is it sufficient for the majority of patients?
cards.back: Fluid and electrolyte repletion is the default management. Most bacterial gastroenteritis is self-limited, so rehydration alone is enough and targeted antibiotics are reserved for specific situations.

cards.front: In cholera, what is the single most lifesaving intervention, and what is the underlying threat that makes it so critical?
cards.back: Aggressive rehydration is lifesaving in cholera. The toxin drives massive secretory fluid loss that can rapidly progress to hypovolemic shock, so replacing volume is the priority over any antimicrobial.

cards.front: Why are antibiotics avoided in enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC) infection, and what is the correct management instead?
cards.back: Antibiotics offer no benefit in EHEC and increase the risk of hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS), likely by enhancing Shiga toxin release. Management is supportive with fluids only.

cards.front: Rotavirus, norovirus, and adenovirus all cause watery diarrhea, so which genome type, affected population, and classic clinical setting distinguish each one?
cards.back: Rotavirus is dsRNA, affects young children, peaks in winter, and is vaccine-preventable. Norovirus is ssRNA, affects adults, and causes cruise-ship and institutional outbreaks. Enteric adenovirus (serotypes 40/41) is the only DNA virus (dsDNA) and affects children and the immunocompromised.

cards.front: What genome type, patient population, season, and toxin mechanism characterize rotavirus gastroenteritis?
cards.back: Rotavirus is a double-stranded RNA virus that classically affects children in winter, with vomiting preceding diarrhea. Its NSP4 protein acts as an enterotoxin causing osmotic diarrhea, and a vaccine exists.

cards.front: What genome type, population, and outbreak setting define norovirus, and is a vaccine available?
cards.back: Norovirus is a single-stranded RNA virus that predominantly affects adults and causes cruise-ship and institutional outbreaks via binding to histo-blood group antigens (HBGA). There is no vaccine.

cards.front: Among the viral causes of gastroenteritis covered, which one has a DNA genome, and what serotypes and population are involved?
cards.back: Enteric adenovirus is the only DNA virus (double-stranded DNA) among these causes. Serotypes 40 and 41 cause gastroenteritis, primarily in children and immunocompromised patients.

cards.front: What is the most sensitive diagnostic test for viral gastroenteritis, and which host binding factor is shared by rotavirus and norovirus?
cards.back: RT-PCR is the most sensitive viral diagnostic test. Histo-blood group antigens (HBGA) enhance the binding of both rotavirus and norovirus, making it a shared susceptibility factor.

cards.front: With no routine antiviral for viral gastroenteritis, what does management actually consist of, and what single preventive tool exists for only one of these viruses?
cards.back: Management is supportive, centered on fluid and electrolyte replacement, because viral gastroenteritis is self-limited and has no routine antiviral. The only preventive tool is the live attenuated rotavirus vaccine; norovirus and adenovirus have no vaccine.

cards.front: What does management of viral gastroenteritis consist of, and why are antivirals not part of routine care?
cards.back: Management is supportive, providing fluids and electrolytes. Viral gastroenteritis is self-limited, so no routine antivirals are used.

cards.front: Among rotavirus, norovirus, and adenovirus, which is vaccine-preventable, and what type of vaccine is it?
cards.back: Only rotavirus is vaccine-preventable, with a live attenuated oral vaccine. Norovirus and adenovirus have no available vaccine.

sections.title: Energy metabolism vocabulary and liver pathways
Regulation of food intake and feeding versus fasting

cards.front: In metabolic vocabulary, what direction of reaction does the suffix -lysis signal versus -genesis, and why does the suffix alone tell you the answer?
cards.back: The suffix -lysis means breaking down or splitting (a catabolic, degrading direction), while -genesis means making or building up (an anabolic, synthetic direction). So glycogenolysis breaks glycogen down whereas glycogenesis builds it, and the suffix reveals the direction before any biochemistry is known.

cards.front: How is the respiratory quotient (RQ) defined in terms of gas exchange?
cards.back: The respiratory quotient is the volume of CO2 released divided by the volume of O2 absorbed.

cards.front: What does basal metabolic rate (BMR) measure, and is it the same for everyone?
cards.back: Basal metabolic rate is the number of calories needed for basic life-sustaining function at rest. It differs from person to person.

cards.front: What distinguishes aerobic metabolism from glycolysis in terms of oxygen use and pathway?
cards.back: Aerobic metabolism is the oxygen-using TCA (Krebs) pathway, whereas glycolysis alone is the anaerobic front end that does not require oxygen.

cards.front: Deep into a fast with hepatic glycogen nearly exhausted but blood glucose still defended, which liver pathways are running and where is the glucose coming from?
cards.back: With glycogen depleted, the liver maintains blood glucose mainly through gluconeogenesis, generating new glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors such as amino acids, glycerol, and lactate. Fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis supply alternative fuel so glucose is spared for the brain.

cards.front: When blood glucose is high, which liver pathway stores glucose as glycogen, and which pathway reverses that storage?
cards.back: Glycogenesis stores excess glucose as glycogen when blood glucose is high, and glycogenolysis reverses the process by breaking glycogen back down to glucose.

cards.front: Which enzyme converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, the molecule that feeds into the TCA cycle?
cards.back: Pyruvate dehydrogenase converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, the convergence molecule that enters the TCA cycle.

cards.front: What metabolic conditions must be met before hepatic ketogenesis is triggered?
cards.back: Ketogenesis fires only when both glucose and glycogen stores are depleted.

cards.front: What happens to carbohydrate intake that exceeds the liver's glycogen storage capacity?
cards.back: Excess carbohydrate beyond glycogen capacity is routed into lipogenesis and stored as triglyceride.

cards.front: Two hepatocytes differ only in their ATP:AMP ratio. From that alone, what can you predict about which metabolic pathways each one is running?
cards.back: The hepatocyte with a high ATP:AMP ratio is energy-replete, so glycolysis is inhibited and storage pathways (glycogenesis and lipogenesis) are favored. The hepatocyte with a low ATP:AMP ratio is energy-depleted, so breakdown pathways are activated to generate ATP.

cards.front: How does the ATP:AMP ratio act as a switch between fuel storage and fuel breakdown?
cards.back: A high ATP:AMP ratio inhibits glycolysis and drives storage pathways (glycogenesis and lipogenesis), while a low ATP:AMP ratio shifts metabolism toward breakdown to regenerate ATP.

cards.front: When the stomach is empty and signaling hunger, trace the hormone from the stomach to the hypothalamic peptide it releases and name the appetite-suppressing signal it shuts off.
cards.back: An empty stomach releases ghrelin, which acts on the hypothalamus to release the orexigenic peptides NPY and AgRP, driving hunger. Ghrelin also inhibits alpha-MSH, the appetite-suppressing (anorexigenic) signal.

cards.front: Through what hypothalamic peptides does ghrelin drive hunger, and which anorexigenic signal does it suppress?
cards.back: Ghrelin, released by the empty stomach, triggers hypothalamic release of NPY and AgRP to produce hunger, and it also inhibits alpha-MSH.

cards.front: Which signals are orexigenic (appetite-stimulating) and which are anorexigenic (appetite-suppressing)?
cards.back: Orexigenic signals include NPY, AgRP, ghrelin, and cortisol. Anorexigenic signals include alpha-MSH, insulin, leptin, GLP-1, CCK, and serotonin.

cards.front: During an overnight fast the brain still receives glucose. Which four hormones make that happen, and which single organ are they all leaning on?
cards.back: Glucagon, epinephrine, cortisol, and growth hormone are the counter-regulatory hormones that defend brain glucose during fasting. They all act on the liver, driving glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to keep glucose available.

cards.front: Which four counter-regulatory hormones defend blood glucose for the brain during fasting?
cards.back: The fasting brain-glucose defenders are glucagon, epinephrine, cortisol, and growth hormone.

cards.front: Beyond their digestive role in the fed state, what additional appetite function do GLP-1 and CCK share?
cards.back: GLP-1 and CCK are fed-state digestive hormones that also act as anorexigenic satiety signals, suppressing further food intake.

cards.front: What does secretin trigger to neutralize chyme, and how does GLP-1 modulate secretin to protect pepsin activation?
cards.back: Secretin stimulates bicarbonate secretion, which neutralizes acidic chyme. GLP-1 inhibits secretin so that gastric acid is preserved, allowing continued activation of pepsin.

cards.front: A protein meal raises both insulin and glucagon, but a carbohydrate meal raises insulin while suppressing glucagon. Why does the body want glucagon up after a steak?
cards.back: A protein-rich, low-carbohydrate meal raises insulin, which would otherwise drive glucose into cells and cause hypoglycemia. The simultaneous rise in glucagon stimulates hepatic glucose output, preventing the blood glucose from falling too low when little carbohydrate has been eaten.
